title: Shell strikes £12bn deal for Canadian gas giant
Blue chips falter as oil price moves ever higher
body: Something went wrong The FTSE 100 closed down on Monday, weighed by falls in Marks & Spencer, J Sainsbury and Shell, as the stalemate in the Middle East dragged on. The FTSE 100 closed down 57.99 points, 0.6%, at 10,321.09. The FTSE 250 ended down just 3.38 points at 22,579.43, and the AIM All-Share fell 1.22 points, 0.2%, at 795.18. Hopes that Iranian foreign minister Abbas Araghchi’s visit to Islamabad would spur fresh negotiations with the US were dashed as US President Donald Trump scrapped a planned trip by envoys on Saturday. Mr Trump told Fox News after calling off his emissaries’ trip that if Iran wanted talks, “they can come to us, or they can call us”. title: Facilities by ADF And 2 Other UK Penny Stocks With Promising Prospects
body: Oops, something went wrong The UK market has recently faced challenges, with the FTSE 100 index experiencing declines due to weak trade data from China, highlighting global economic interdependencies. Despite these broader market pressures, investors often look for opportunities in areas that can offer growth potential. Penny stocks, though an older term, continue to represent a viable investment area by providing access to smaller or newer companies that might combine affordability with strong financials and long-term prospects.
